title: Wu Chengying

22 April 2001 | [Shaanxi Province Stadium](/wiki/Shaanxi_Province_Stadium "Shaanxi Province Stadium"), [Xi'an](/wiki/Xi%27an "Xi'an"), [China](/wiki/China "China") | [Maldives](/wiki/Maldives_national_football_team "Maldives national football team") | **2**–0 | 10–1 | [2002 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_AFC_first_round "2002 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC first round")

27 May 2001 | [Gelora Bung Karno](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium "Gelora Bung Karno Stadium"), [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") | [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia_national_football_team "Indonesia national football team") | **2**–0 | 2–0 | 2002 FIFA World Cup qualification

([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 吴承瑛; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Wú Chéngyīng*; born 21 April 1975 in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai")) is a Chinese former international football player who spent the majority of his career at [Shanghai Shenhua](/wiki/Shanghai_Shenhua_F.C. "Shanghai Shenhua F.C.") where he won the league and [Chinese FA Cup](/wiki/Chinese_FA_Cup "Chinese FA Cup") before moving to [Shanghai International](/wiki/Shanghai_International "Shanghai International") for a then Chinese record fee of 13,000,000 [RMB](/wiki/RMB "RMB") in 2003. Internationally he was a member of the [Chinese national team](/wiki/China_national_football_team "China national football team") where he participated in the [1996](/wiki/1996_AFC_Asian_Cup "1996 AFC Asian Cup") and [2000 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2000_AFC_Asian_Cup "2000 AFC Asian Cup") as well also the [2002 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup").
["吴承瑛低调复出投身青训:过去十年是瘫痪局面"](http://sports.qq.com/a/20150609/045576.htm). sports.qq.com. 9 June 2015. Retrieved 6 January 2018.

["FIFA Tournaments - Players & Coaches - WU Chengying"](https://web.archive.org/web/20080619045240/http://www.fifa.com/worldfootball/statisticsandrecords/players/player=159603/index.html). Archived from [the original](https://www.fifa.com/worldfootball/statisticsandrecords/players/player=159603/index.html) on 19 June 2008. Retrieved 19 October 2016.

["Wu wows 'em"](https://web.archive.org/web/19980514215357/http://www.asian-football.com/afc/pub/April98/frame.r4.html). *[Asian Football Confederation](/wiki/Asian_Football_Confederation "Asian Football Confederation")*. 14 May 1998. Archived from [the original](http://www.asian-football.com/afc/pub/April98/frame.r4.html) on 14 May 1998.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Chinese footballer (born 1975)

Wu Chengying   
吴承瑛

| Personal information | | | |

| Medal record  |  |  | | --- | --- | |  | | | Men's [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") | | | | Representing  [China](/wiki/China "China") | | | | [AFC U-16 Championship](/wiki/AFC_U-17_Asian_Cup "AFC U-17 Asian Cup") | | | | Bronze medal – third place | [1990 UAE](/wiki/1990_AFC_U-16_Championship "1990 AFC U-16 Championship") | [Team](/wiki/China_national_under-17_football_team "China national under-17 football team") | | | | |

**Wu Chengying** ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 吴承瑛; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Wú Chéngyīng*; born 21 April 1975 in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai")) is a Chinese former international football player who spent the majority of his career at [Shanghai Shenhua](/wiki/Shanghai_Shenhua_F.C. "Shanghai Shenhua F.C.") where he won the league and [Chinese FA Cup](/wiki/Chinese_FA_Cup "Chinese FA Cup") before moving to [Shanghai International](/wiki/Shanghai_International "Shanghai International") for a then Chinese record fee of 13,000,000 [RMB](/wiki/RMB "RMB") in 2003. Internationally he was a member of the [Chinese national team](/wiki/China_national_football_team "China national football team") where he participated in the [1996](/wiki/1996_AFC_Asian_Cup "1996 AFC Asian Cup") and [2000 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2000_AFC_Asian_Cup "2000 AFC Asian Cup") as well also the [2002 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup").

## Playing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wu_Chengying&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Playing career")]

Wu Chengying emerged with future Chinese internationals [Shen Si](/wiki/Shen_Si "Shen Si"), [Xie Hui](/wiki/Xie_Hui_(footballer) "Xie Hui (footballer)") and [Qi Hong](/wiki/Qi_Hong "Qi Hong") form [Shanghai Shenhua](/wiki/Shanghai_Shenhua_F.C. "Shanghai Shenhua F.C.") where he was predominantly used in the left back position.[[1]](#cite_note-sina_wu_chengying-1) During his spell at Shanghai Shenhua the team claimed the [1995 Chinese Jia-A League](/wiki/Chinese_Jia-A_League_1995 "Chinese Jia-A League 1995") title and the 1998 [Chinese FA Cup](/wiki/Chinese_FA_Cup "Chinese FA Cup"). In February 1998, he was loaned to [Dalian Wanda](/wiki/Dalian_Shide_F.C. "Dalian Shide F.C.") for half a month for the [1997–98 Asian Club Championship](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_Asian_Club_Championship "1997–98 Asian Club Championship") and scored a goal against [Pohang Steelers](/wiki/Pohang_Steelers "Pohang Steelers"). He attracted the interests of Italian side [Calcio Como](/wiki/Calcio_Como "Calcio Como") in January 2002 but the [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") club failed to reach an agreement with Shanghai Shenhua. Wu Chengying instead joined [Shanghai International](/wiki/Beijing_Chengfeng_F.C. "Beijing Chengfeng F.C.") later in 2003 for 13,000,000 [RMB](/wiki/RMB "RMB"), which made him the highest Chinese transfer ever in Chinese football history.[[1]](#cite_note-sina_wu_chengying-1) At Shanghai International he would see them come second in the 2003 league season and third in the following campaign.[[2]](#cite_note-2) When his contract expired at the end of the 2006 league season, Wu decided to announce his retirement from playing.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## International career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wu_Chengying&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: International career")]

After his performances for Shanghai Shenhua saw him win the league title he was included on the squad that went to the [1996 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/1996_AFC_Asian_Cup "1996 AFC Asian Cup") and became a regular within the team. During [Bora Milutinović](/wiki/Bora_Milutinovi%C4%87 "Bora Milutinović")'s reign as the Chinese head coach Wu Chengying became China's first choice left back despite having an indifferent relationship with several regular players such as [Ou Chuliang](/wiki/Ou_Chuliang "Ou Chuliang") and [Li Jinyu](/wiki/Li_Jinyu "Li Jinyu"), who described him as extremely introverted.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Despite his indifferent relationship with his teammates Wu was a regular starter for the national team playing in the [2000 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2000_AFC_Asian_Cup "2000 AFC Asian Cup") and [2002 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup") until Bora Milutinović's reign as the Chinese national team ended.[[5]](#cite_note-5)
